content.text: Streaming Data Pipelines with Apache Kafka in Python

Modern applications generate data continuously — user clicks, sensor readings, financial transactions, log events — and batch processing simply cannot keep up. Streaming pipelines process data the moment it arrives, enabling real-time analytics, fraud detection, and live dashboards. Apache Kafka is the industry-standard backbone for these pipelines, acting as a high-throughput, fault-tolerant message broker that decouples producers of data from consumers of data.

is a distributed event streaming platform originally developed at LinkedIn and open-sourced in 2011. It is designed to handle millions of events per second with low latency, durable storage, and horizontal scalability.
built on Kafka consists of three logical layers:
— Applications that publish (write) events into Kafka.
— The distributed storage and routing layer that holds events in ordered, immutable logs.
— Applications that subscribe to and process those events, often writing results to databases, dashboards, or downstream services.
In the Python ecosystem, the two primary Kafka client libraries are:
— Python bindings around the high-performance C library
, preferred in production for throughput and reliability.
Traditional message queues (like RabbitMQ) delete messages once consumed. Kafka is fundamentally different — it stores events as an
on disk, retained for a configurable period (hours, days, or indefinitely). This enables:
reading the same data at their own pace.
— a new service can read from the beginning of a topic.
— if a consumer crashes, it resumes from where it left off.
— producers and consumers evolve independently without knowing about each other.
This makes Kafka ideal for building streaming pipelines where data flows continuously from many sources to many destinations.
Understanding Kafka's internals is essential before writing any code.
is a named, logical channel for a category of events (e.g.,
). Each topic is split into one or more
— ordered, immutable sequences of records stored on disk.

Topic: "payments"
  Partition 0: [msg0, msg1, msg2, msg3, ...]
  Partition 1: [msg0, msg1, msg2, ...]
  Partition 2: [msg0, msg1, msg2, msg3, msg4, ...]
Each message within a partition has a unique integer
. Offsets increase monotonically. Messages across partitions are not globally ordered, but within a single partition, ordering is guaranteed.
Partitions enable parallelism. Multiple consumer instances can each read from a different partition simultaneously, scaling throughput linearly.
A producer sends a record to a topic. If a
to determine the partition. All records with the same key go to the same partition, guaranteeing ordering for that key. Without a key, records are distributed round-robin across partitions.
This is critical for use cases like order events — if all events for
must be processed in order, use

is a set of consumer instances that collectively read a topic. Kafka assigns each partition to exactly one consumer in a group. This is how Kafka achieves parallel consumption while ensuring each record is processed exactly once within a group.

Topic "payments" with 3 partitions:
  Consumer Group "payment-processor" (3 instances):
    Instance A → Partition 0
    Instance B → Partition 1
    Instance C → Partition 2
If you add a fourth consumer to a group with three partitions, one consumer sits idle. Conversely, fewer consumers than partitions means some consumers handle multiple partitions.
) reads the same topic from the beginning independently — it has its own offset tracking.
for each consumer group. When a consumer processes a record, it commits the offset to tell Kafka "I've processed up to here." If the consumer restarts, it resumes from the last committed offset.
(enabled by default) commits offsets periodically on a timer.
gives precise control — commit only after successfully processing, preventing data loss on crash.
broker (handles all reads/writes) and zero or more
(typically 3 in production) determines how many copies exist. If the leader broker fails, a replica is promoted.
— Fire and forget. No confirmation. Fastest, but data can be lost.
) — All in-sync replicas confirm write. Strongest durability guarantee.
Architecture of a Python Streaming Pipeline
— Producers read from external sources (REST APIs, databases, IoT sensors) and write raw events to a Kafka topic.
— Consumers read raw events, clean/enrich them (parse JSON, join with reference data, filter invalid records), and produce transformed events to a new topic.
— Final consumers write to a database, search index, data warehouse, or real-time dashboard.
This pattern — source → transform → sink — is sometimes called an
in the JVM ecosystem. In Python, the transforms are plain code in consumer loops.

# Lightweight, pure Python (good for learning and low-volume use)
pip install kafka-python

# Production-grade (wraps librdkafka, much higher throughput)
pip install confluent-kafka
Setting Up Kafka Locally with Docker
The fastest way to run Kafka locally for development:

# docker-compose.yml
version: "3"
services:
  zookeeper:
    image: confluentinc/cp-zookeeper:7.5.0
    environment:
      ZOOKEEPER_CLIENT_PORT: 2181

  kafka:
    image: confluentinc/cp-kafka:7.5.0
    depends_on:
      - zookeeper
    ports:
      - "9092:9092"
    environment:
      KAFKA_BROKER_ID: 1
      KAFKA_ZOOKEEPER_CONNECT: zookeeper:2181
      KAFKA_ADVERTISED_LISTENERS: PLAINTEXT://localhost:9092
      KAFKA_OFFSETS_TOPIC_REPLICATION_FACTOR: 1
Kafka 3.x+ supports running without Zookeeper using
. The above config uses the traditional setup which remains common in tutorials.

import json
import time
from kafka import KafkaProducer

producer = KafkaProducer(
    bootstrap_servers=["localhost:9092"],
    # Serialize values to JSON bytes
    value_serializer=lambda v: json.dumps(v).encode("utf-8"),
    # Serialize keys to UTF-8 bytes
    key_serializer=lambda k: k.encode("utf-8") if k else None,
    # Durability: wait for all in-sync replicas
    acks="all",
    # Retry on transient failures
    retries=3,
)

def simulate_payment_events():
    for i in range(100):
        event = {
            "payment_id": f"pay_{i}",
            "user_id": f"user_{i % 10}",
            "amount": round(100 + i * 1.5, 2),
            "currency": "USD",
            "timestamp": time.time(),
        }
        # Key by user_id so all events for a user go to the same partition
        future = producer.send(
            topic="payments",
            key=event["user_id"],
            value=event,
        )
        # Optional: block and get RecordMetadata
        record_metadata = future.get(timeout=10)
        print(
            f"Sent to partition={record_metadata.partition}, "
            f"offset={record_metadata.offset}"
        )
        time.sleep(0.1)

    producer.flush()  # Ensure all buffered messages are sent
    producer.close()

simulate_payment_events()

import json
import time
from confluent_kafka import Producer
from confluent_kafka.admin import AdminClient, NewTopic

# --- Create topic programmatically ---
admin = AdminClient({"bootstrap.servers": "localhost:9092"})
topic = NewTopic("payments", num_partitions=3, replication_factor=1)
admin.create_topics([topic])

# --- Producer ---
producer = Producer({
    "bootstrap.servers": "localhost:9092",
    "acks": "all",
    "retries": 3,
    "enable.idempotence": True,  # Exactly-once semantics at producer level
})

def delivery_report(err, msg):
    """Callback invoked once per produced message."""
    if err is not None:
        print(f"Delivery failed: {err}")
    else:
        print(f"Delivered to {msg.topic()} [{msg.partition()}] @ offset {msg.offset()}")

for i in range(100):
    event = {
        "payment_id": f"pay_{i}",
        "user_id": f"user_{i % 10}",
        "amount": round(100 + i * 1.5, 2),
        "timestamp": time.time(),
    }
    producer.produce(
        topic="payments",
        key=f"user_{i % 10}",
        value=json.dumps(event).encode("utf-8"),
        callback=delivery_report,
    )
    # Poll triggers delivery callbacks; call frequently to avoid buffer overflow
    producer.poll(0)

producer.flush()
ensures that retried messages are not duplicated — Kafka assigns each producer a unique ID and sequence number, detecting and discarding duplicates automatically.

import json
from kafka import KafkaConsumer

consumer = KafkaConsumer(
    "payments",
    bootstrap_servers=["localhost:9092"],
    group_id="payment-processor",        # Consumer group name
    auto_offset_reset="earliest",        # Start from beginning if no committed offset
    enable_auto_commit=False,            # Manual commit for reliability
    value_deserializer=lambda b: json.loads(b.decode("utf-8")),
    key_deserializer=lambda b: b.decode("utf-8") if b else None,
    max_poll_records=100,                # Max records per poll call
)

def process_payment(event: dict) -> None:
    """Business logic: validate, enrich, write to DB, etc."""
    amount = event["amount"]
    if amount > 1000:
        print(f"[ALERT] Large transaction: {event['payment_id']} — ${amount}")
    else:
        print(f"[OK] Processed: {event['payment_id']} — ${amount}")

try:
    for message in consumer:
        event = message.value
        try:
            process_payment(event)
            # Commit only after successful processing
            consumer.commit()
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Error processing {event}: {e}")
            # Do NOT commit — message will be redelivered after restart
finally:
    consumer.close()
Building a Full Transform Pipeline (Producer + Consumer Chained)
A real streaming pipeline usually chains stages. A consumer reads from
, transforms each record, and a producer writes results to

is critical. If reversed, a crash between commit and produce would silently drop data. This approach may produce duplicates on crash (at-least-once semantics), but never loses data. True exactly-once requires Kafka transactions (covered below).
JSON is human-readable but verbose and slow. Production pipelines use binary serialization formats.
is a compact binary format with a schema. The
stores schemas centrally so that producers and consumers can independently evolve their data formats while remaining compatible.

from confluent_kafka import avro
from confluent_kafka.avro import AvroProducer, AvroConsumer

SCHEMA_STR = """
{
  "type": "record",
  "name": "Payment",
  "fields": [
    {"name": "payment_id", "type": "string"},
    {"name": "user_id",    "type": "string"},
    {"name": "amount",     "type": "double"},
    {"name": "timestamp",  "type": "double"}
  ]
}
"""

value_schema = avro.loads(SCHEMA_STR)

producer = AvroProducer(
    {
        "bootstrap.servers": "localhost:9092",
        "schema.registry.url": "http://localhost:8081",
    },
    default_value_schema=value_schema,
)

producer.produce(
    topic="payments",
    value={"payment_id": "pay_1", "user_id": "user_5", "amount": 250.0, "timestamp": 1700000000.0},
)
producer.flush()
Avro payloads are typically 5–10x smaller than equivalent JSON, and schema validation catches breaking changes before they reach production.
: if a consumer crashes after processing but before committing, the message is redelivered and processed again. For financial or idempotency-sensitive pipelines, Kafka supports

from confluent_kafka import Producer, Consumer, KafkaException

producer = Producer({
    "bootstrap.servers": "localhost:9092",
    "transactional.id": "payment-enricher-txn-1",  # Unique per producer instance
    "enable.idempotence": True,
})

consumer = Consumer({
    "bootstrap.servers": "localhost:9092",
    "group.id": "payment-enricher-eos",
    "enable.auto.commit": False,
    "isolation.level": "read_committed",  # Only read committed messages
})

producer.init_transactions()
consumer.subscribe(["raw-payments"])

try:
    while True:
        msg = consumer.poll(1.0)
        if msg is None or msg.error():
            continue

        raw_event = json.loads(msg.value().decode("utf-8"))
        enriched = enrich_payment(raw_event)

        try:
            producer.begin_transaction()

            producer.produce(
                topic="enriched-payments",
                key=msg.key(),
                value=json.dumps(enriched).encode("utf-8"),
            )

            # Commit consumer offset as part of the same transaction
            producer.send_offsets_to_transaction(
                consumer.position(consumer.assignment()),
                consumer.consumer_group_metadata(),
            )

            producer.commit_transaction()  # Atomic: produce + offset commit together

        except KafkaException:
            producer.abort_transaction()
            # Message will be redelivered; do not advance consumer

except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass
finally:
    consumer.close()
With EOS, the produce and the offset commit happen atomically in a single Kafka transaction. Either both succeed or neither does. Consumers reading with
only see records from committed transactions, never partial or aborted ones.
For high-throughput pipelines, blocking I/O is a bottleneck. The
library provides asyncio-native Kafka producers and consumers.

import asyncio
import json
from aiokafka import AIOKafkaProducer, AIOKafkaConsumer

BOOTSTRAP = "localhost:9092"

async def produce_events():
    producer = AIOKafkaProducer(
        bootstrap_servers=BOOTSTRAP,
        value_serializer=lambda v: json.dumps(v).encode("utf-8"),
    )
    await producer.start()
    try:
        for i in range(50):
            await producer.send_and_wait(
                "payments",
                value={"payment_id": f"pay_{i}", "amount": i * 10.0},
            )
    finally:
        await producer.stop()

async def consume_events():
    consumer = AIOKafkaConsumer(
        "payments",
        bootstrap_servers=BOOTSTRAP,
        group_id="async-processor",
        auto_offset_reset="earliest",
        value_deserializer=lambda b: json.loads(b.decode("utf-8")),
    )
    await consumer.start()
    try:
        async for msg in consumer:
            event = msg.value
            print(f"Received: {event['payment_id']}")
            await asyncio.sleep(0)  # Yield to event loop
    finally:
        await consumer.stop()

async def main():
    await asyncio.gather(produce_events(), consume_events())

asyncio.run(main())
is particularly valuable when consumers call async downstream services (HTTP APIs, async database clients) — the event loop handles many in-flight requests concurrently without spawning threads.
Not every message will process successfully. Rather than crashing or silently dropping failed messages, a
receives messages that cannot be processed, where they can be inspected and retried manually.

def safe_process(consumer, producer, msg):
    raw_event = json.loads(msg.value().decode("utf-8"))
    try:
        result = enrich_payment(raw_event)
        producer.produce("enriched-payments", value=json.dumps(result).encode())
    except Exception as e:
        # Publish to DLQ with error metadata
        dlq_record = {
            "original_topic": msg.topic(),
            "original_partition": msg.partition(),
            "original_offset": msg.offset(),
            "original_value": raw_event,
            "error": str(e),
        }
        producer.produce("payments-dlq", value=json.dumps(dlq_record).encode())
        print(f"Sent to DLQ: {raw_event['payment_id']}")

    producer.poll(0)
    consumer.commit(message=msg)
A DLQ makes pipelines operationally robust — bad messages do not block the pipeline, and engineers can investigate, fix the processing logic, and replay from the DLQ.
When to Use Kafka (and When Not To)
Event volume is high (thousands to millions per second).
Multiple independent consumers need the same data.
Events must be replayed or audited after the fact.
Producers and consumers need to evolve independently.
You need durable buffering between fast producers and slow consumers.
Event volume is low and a simple job queue suffices (use Celery + Redis or RQ).
You need request-reply (RPC) semantics — Kafka is one-way.
The operational complexity of a Kafka cluster outweighs the benefit (for small teams or low-traffic apps, managed queues like AWS SQS are simpler).
You need complex streaming SQL without writing consumer code — consider Kafka Streams (JVM) or ksqlDB.
— Auto-commit advances the offset on a timer regardless of whether processing succeeded. A crash between commit and processing silently loses data. Always use manual commit in production.

— Committing immediately after receiving a message but before completing the business logic causes data loss on crash. Always commit only after the processing step and any downstream writes are complete.
— A topic with one partition can only be consumed by one consumer instance. Always partition topics appropriately for the expected consumer parallelism (3–12 partitions is a common starting point).
— Producers buffer messages internally for batching. Without
before shutdown, buffered messages are silently discarded. Always
Deserializing inside the poll loop without error handling
— A single malformed message can crash the entire consumer loop. Always wrap deserialization in try/except and route bad messages to a DLQ.
Too many or too few partitions
— More partitions increase parallelism but also increase broker overhead, rebalancing time, and file descriptor usage. Fewer partitions limit throughput. Benchmark and right-size for actual load.
— If the consumer starts after the producer has run, it will miss all historical messages. Use
— If producers outpace consumers, messages accumulate in Kafka indefinitely.
(the difference between the latest offset and the committed offset) is the key metric to monitor.
— Choose message keys deliberately. Use entity IDs (user_id, order_id) so all events for an entity land in the same partition and are processed in order.
— Design processing logic so that re-processing a duplicate message produces the same result. This makes at-least-once delivery safe in practice (upsert to database instead of insert, for example).
Separate topics by schema and lifecycle
— Avoid putting heterogeneous event types in a single topic. One topic per event type simplifies schema management and consumer logic.
— Default retention is 7 days. For audit-critical data, increase to 30–90 days or use compacted topics (Kafka Log Compaction keeps the latest value per key indefinitely).
— Enforce schema compatibility (backward, forward, or full) at the registry level. This prevents breaking changes from reaching consumers silently.
per partition and group — primary health signal.
— Kafka can silently run out of disk.
— signals replication lag or broker failure.
to allow more records to be batched into a single network request. This trades tiny latency for significantly higher throughput.
Kafka's throughput scales horizontally. Adding brokers and partitions increases write and read throughput nearly linearly.
A single Kafka broker on modern hardware can sustain:
read throughput (reads are served from the OS page cache, not disk).
at high throughput — benchmarks commonly show 3–5x higher message rates.
(each in its own OS process) rather than threads, to avoid the Python GIL.
For I/O-bound processing (writing to databases, calling APIs), use
with asyncio for concurrency within a single process.
— instead of processing one message at a time, collect a batch from
and process in bulk (useful for batch database inserts).
Python stream processing framework (like Kafka Streams for Python)
Stateful stream processing at massive scale (Java/Python API)
Pre-built connectors — stream from databases, S3, Elasticsearch without writing code
SQL interface for stream processing on Kafka topics
Centralized schema management for Avro/Protobuf/JSON Schema
Open-source web UIs for browsing topics, consumer groups, and lag
Monitoring broker metrics and consumer lag dashboards
Kafka-compatible broker written in C++ — much lower resource usage, simpler ops

deserves special mention for Python teams — it is a Python library for writing Kafka stream processors as idiomatic Python code, with built-in support for stateful processing, windowed aggregations, and table joins.

import faust

app = faust.App("payment-enricher", broker="kafka://localhost:9092")

raw_topic = app.topic("raw-payments", value_type=dict)
enriched_topic = app.topic("enriched-payments", value_type=dict)

@app.agent(raw_topic)
async def enrich(payments):
    async for payment in payments:
        enriched = {**payment, "is_large": payment["amount"] > 1000}
        await enriched_topic.send(value=enriched)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.main()

Kafka's primary advantages are replay, high throughput, multi-consumer fan-out, and the ability to hold events for extended periods. Its primary disadvantage is operational complexity — running a healthy Kafka cluster requires understanding replication, partition management, and ZooKeeper/KRaft. Managed offerings (Confluent Cloud, AWS MSK, Redpanda Cloud) eliminate most of this burden.
— Kafka supports SASL/PLAIN, SASL/SCRAM, and mTLS (mutual TLS). Always authenticate in production.
— Kafka ACLs control which service accounts can produce to or consume from which topics. Use the principle of least privilege — a consumer service should not have produce rights.
— Enable TLS in transit. Kafka does not encrypt data at rest natively; use encrypted disks (LUKS, AWS EBS encryption) for at-rest protection.
— Always use replication factor 3 in production. With RF=1, a single broker failure causes data loss.
— Set to 2 when RF=3. Combined with
, this ensures writes succeed only when at least 2 replicas have confirmed, preventing data loss even if one replica is temporarily behind.

— When a consumer crashes mid-batch, the next consumer reads from the last committed offset. This is automatic and handled by Kafka's group coordinator. Ensure processing is idempotent so safe re-delivery causes no harm.

— If consumers fall behind, Kafka buffers the excess in its log (up to the retention limit). Unlike in-memory queues, Kafka does not crash under backpressure — it simply retains messages on disk until consumers catch up.
— not a traditional message queue. Events persist and can be replayed by any number of independent consumers.
are the unit of parallelism: more partitions allow more consumers to work simultaneously, but require careful key design to maintain ordering guarantees.
allow multiple independent applications to read the same data independently; within a group, each partition is owned by exactly one consumer.
is the correct pattern for reliability — auto-commit risks silent data loss on crash.
in production for throughput and reliability; use
are the path to exactly-once semantics when correctness is non-negotiable.
are non-optional in any serious production deployment.
Python streaming pipelines are most scalable when CPU-bound work uses multiple processes and I/O-bound work uses
